stories.title: Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: Glasshouse has signed a long-term lease for 66,436 square feet at 3 World Trade Center. The 30-year agreement for a portion of the ground floor and the entirety of the second and third floors brings the event, hospitality and experiential venue operator to the World Trade Center campus for its fourth Manhattan location. CBRE’s Chris Mansfield, Anthony Dattoma and Zachary Weil represented Glasshouse. The transaction involved a three-party structure among Glasshouse, Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (URW) and the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. URW represented itself. “This transaction represents one of the most significant experiential leasing transactions completed in Manhattan in recent years,” said Dattoma. “Beyond creating a flagship venue for Glasshouse, this lease complements the continued momentum and surge of leasing activity across Lower Manhattan. The new venue is expected to attract more than 250,000 visitors annually, bringing additional energy and activation to the World Trade Center campus while further strengthening Downtown as one of New York City’s premier destinations for business, hospitality, culture and community engagement.” The post Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Glasshouse signs 30-year, 66,436-sf lease at 3 World Trade Center, Lower Manhattan.
Event operator expected to draw 250,000+ annual visitors to Downtown campus.
Represents major experiential leasing activity, signals ongoing Lower Manhattan momentum.
stories.summary: Glasshouse, an events and hospitality operator, has committed to a 30-year lease of 66,436 square feet across ground, second, and third floors at 3 World Trade Center in Lower Manhattan. The transaction, brokered by CBRE, involves three parties including URW and the Port Authority. The venue is expected to attract over 250,000 visitors annually and represents one of Manhattan's most significant experiential leasing deals in recent years, reinforcing Downtown's recovery.
stories.impact: While primarily office/experiential leasing, this signals sustained demand and foot traffic into Lower Manhattan's mixed-use ecosystem, which can support stronger residential demand in adjacent neighborhoods (Battery Park, Financial District submarkets) as people relocate closer to revitalized employment and entertainment nodes. The 250k annual visitors also indicates broader economic vitality that supports nearby multifamily rents.

stories.title: Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement
stories.excerpt: Northmarq’s Los Angeles Investment Sales team of Mike Hanassab, Steven Goldstein and Elliot Hassan arranged the $5.25-million sale of Hyperion Residences, a three-parcel development site located at 835 N. Hyperion Ave. in Los Angeles’ Silver Lake neighborhood. Northmarq represented the seller, Hyperion Apartments LP. The buyer is a locally based affordable housing developer. The site was delivered fully entitled and Ready-to-Issue for a six-story, approximately 54-unit project under Transit Oriented Communities Tier 3. During escrow, the buyer leveraged Executive Directive 1 to re-entitle the parcels for 105 units, increasing density and reducing per-unit cost significantly. The community is now under construction as a mixed-income development. “Silver Lake remains one of Los Angeles’ most entitlement-constrained corridors, and sites that pencil for Executive Directive 1 projects are increasingly sought after,” said Hanassab. “The buyer recognized the deeper opportunity and moved swiftly to re-entitle the parcels during escrow, bringing their basis to roughly $50,000 per buildable unit — an exceptional outcome for both parties.” The post Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Silver Lake site re-entitled from 54 to 105 units using Executive Directive 1 during escrow.
Buyer achieved ~$50,000/buildable unit basis through density leverage.
Los Angeles entitlement-constrained market; affordable housing developer secured mixed-income project.
stories.summary: Northmarq arranged the $5.25 million sale of Hyperion Residences, a three-parcel development site in Silver Lake, to a local affordable housing developer. The site was initially entitled for 54 units but the buyer leveraged Executive Directive 1 to re-entitle for 105 units during escrow, reducing per-unit basis to approximately $50,000 and enabling mixed-income construction. The move exemplifies the scarcity of entitlement-ready sites in Silver Lake.
stories.impact: Executive Directive 1 re-entitlements signal California's regulatory momentum toward density; developers acquiring LA sites now must model aggressive upzoning into underwriting. Silver Lake's tight entitlement environment means land basis is rising; competing for density-capable parcels requires speed and capital agility. This also suggests affordable/mixed-income projects are increasingly the viable path to entitlement in constrained, gentrifying neighborhoods—shifting development economics away from pure-market-rate models.

stories.title: Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside
stories.excerpt: Construction has begun at 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., a 19-story condominium rising at the border of Sunnyside. The development is led by Hiwin Group USA, whose previous work includes 300 W. 30th St. in Chelsea, a 16-story, 69-residence condo project that sold out in nine months. Scheduled for delivery in 2029, 51-02 Roosevelt Ave. represents a rare high-rise addition to the neighborhood, according to Acre NY Realty, which is serving as sales and marketing representative.”51-02 Roosevelt Ave. is more than a new residential building. It is a meaningful addition to the Sunnyside community,” said Cathy Huang, CEO of Acre NY Realty. “With its height, visibility program and strong transit access, this project has the potential to become a true neighborhood landmark.” The project will include 84 residential units, including 70 one-bedroom and 14 two-bedroom residences. It will also provide ground-floor retail, commercial office space, community-oriented medical space, residential recreation areas, terraces, and parking access. The post Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hiwin Group USA broke ground on 19-story, 84-unit condo at 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., Sunnyside.
Scheduled delivery 2029; includes 70 one-bedrooms, 14 two-bedrooms, ground retail/office.
Rare high-rise addition to neighborhood; developer's prior Chelsea project sold out in 9 months.
stories.summary: Construction has commenced on 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., a 19-story condominium in Sunnyside led by Hiwin Group USA, with delivery targeted for 2029. The project comprises 84 units (70 one-bedrooms, 14 two-bedrooms) plus ground-floor retail, office, and medical space. Hiwin previously developed 300 W. 30th St. in Chelsea, a 69-unit condo that sold out in nine months, demonstrating strong market traction for its product.
stories.impact: This condo groundbreak in Sunnyside (Queens) reflects developer confidence in outer-borough supply absorption and pricing power near transit; strong Chelsea pre-sales data validates Hiwin's model. For rental developers, this signals competitive pressure in Queens as condo-conversion and new-build condo pipelines tighten supply. Sunnyside's relative affordability vs. Manhattan, combined with transit access, is attracting capital; rental developers should monitor whether condo pipelines reduce available multifamily land or accelerate rents.

stories.title: Midwood Buys Mixed-Use Building Across From Former Downtown Brooklyn Macy’s
stories.excerpt: A four-story office and retail building across the street from the former Macy’s department store in Downtown Brooklyn has traded, Commercial Observer has learned. Midwood Investment & Development, a New York City-based real estate development and management firm, purchased 423 Fulton Street — opposite the former Macy’s at 422 Fulton Street — for $9.5 million, according to broker KSR. The property on the corner of Fulton and Pearl streets is home to Panda Express in the ground-floor retail space and Staywell Medical Group in the office space. The seller of 423 Fulton Street is BNN Fulton Flushing Owner, an LLC comprising several parties, including real estate investor Ralph Braha and the Nakash family, which owns Jordache Enterprises. The Nakash family also owns the historic Versace Mansion in Miami Beach, Fla., where it just secured a $44.7 million refinancing loan in May. KSR’s Albert Sultan and Joseph Ash brokered the 423 Fulton Street deal for both the buyer and the seller entity. A spokesperson for Midwood did not immediately respond to a request for comment, while a spokesperson for Jordache could not be reached for comment. While Midwood’s exact plans for its new property are unclear, the deal comes as Downtown Brooklyn experiences a bit of a retail renaissance, especially following news that the neighborhood’s former Macy’s department store will be redeveloped into a five-floor retail and amusement destination called BKX, as CO first reported. Owners United American Land and Jackson Group plan to transform the 440,000-square-foot space at 422 Fulton Street into a hot spot for large-format retail tenants, a wellness or fitness facility, and food and beverage companies. And while there’s still 125 available storefronts in Downtown Brooklyn, the area has found success in retail that meets its population’s needs, including three new supermarkets on Fulton Street, Regina Myer, president of the Downtown Brooklyn Partnership, said during CO’s 2026 Brooklyn Foru
bullets: Midwood Investment & Development purchased 423 Fulton Street, Downtown Brooklyn, for $9.5M.
Four-story office/retail property; tenants include Panda Express, Staywell Medical Group.
Asset across from former Macy's; signals investment repositioning in revitalizing Downtown corridor.
stories.summary: Midwood Investment & Development acquired 423 Fulton Street in Downtown Brooklyn for $9.5 million, a four-story mixed-use building located opposite the former Macy's. The property houses Panda Express retail and Staywell Medical Group office space. The seller was an LLC that included investor Ralph Braha and the Nakash family (Jordache/Versace Mansion owners).
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, the acquisition reflects investor appetite for repositioning in Downtown Brooklyn's mixed-use node near the former Macy's—a potential anchor for broader neighborhood densification. As Brooklyn continues cultural/retail resurgence, ground-floor retail and office conversion opportunities may emerge; multifamily developers should track Fulton Street's repositioning for future adaptive-reuse or assemblage targets.

stories.title: JPMorgan Funnels $205M to Refi Blackstone’s East Hotel in Miami
stories.excerpt: Blackstone Real Estate has once again landed debt from J.P. Morgan Chase to refinance a South Florida hotel. The bank supplied a $205 million loan backed by the East Miami hotel in the Brickell district, property records show. The 39-story hotel houses 352 rooms, including 89 serviced apartments, a 20,000-square-foot pool deck and the Sugar rooftop bar, located within the 5 million-square-foot Brickell City Centre mixed-use complex at 788 Brickell Plaza. New York-based private equity giant Blackstone purchased the 536,070-square-foot asset for about $300 million last year from Trinity Investments and Certares Real Estate Management. The refinancing appears to be the first loan Blackstone has loaded onto the property, which was completed in 2016. East Miami is one of three hotels in the luxury East brand. The others are in Hong Kong and Beijing. Just last month, J.P. Morgan Chase funneled $115 million to refinance Blackstone’s oceanfront W hotel in Fort Lauderdale. The firm had purchased the 346-room asset for $153 million in 2024. The financing comes on the back of other major refinancings in South Florida. In April, Trinity Investments and UBS nabbed $600 million for the 1,000-room, oceanfront Diplomat Beach Resort in Hollywood also from J.P. Morgan Chase and Citi Real Estate Funding. Representatives for Blackstone did not immediately provide comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: JPMorgan provided $205M refi for Blackstone's 352-room East Miami hotel, Brickell.
Property completed 2016; includes 89 serviced apartments, 20k-sf pool, rooftop bar.
Part of 5M-sf Brickell City Centre; Blackstone purchased for ~$300M in 2023.
stories.summary: Blackstone Real Estate obtained a $205 million refinancing loan from JPMorgan Chase for the East Miami hotel in Brickell, a 352-room luxury property (including 89 serviced apartments) completed in 2016. The property anchors Brickell City Centre, a 5 million-square-foot mixed-use complex. Blackstone acquired the asset in 2023 for approximately $300 million and this marks its first debt load.
stories.impact: Luxury hospitality refinancing signals strong lender appetite for Brickell's mixed-use ecosystem; however, limited direct multifamily relevance. The presence of serviced apartments within the hotel suggests potential for operator-driven hybrid models in dense urban cores—a trend multifamily developers may monitor for differentiation (co-living, flex-stay hybrids). Brickell's density and hospitality investment reinforce its appeal as a residential magnet for younger, service-oriented demographics.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of an industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Rd. in Middleton, MA. The asset sold for $2,525,000. “The quality of this industrial condo drove interest from owner-users and investors,” said Mattias Edenkrans, associate director investments in Marcus & Millichap’s Boston office. “Ultimately, the low market vacancy in this core North Shore market appealed to an investor group that purchased with conviction, allowing us to achieve certainty of execution for our client.” Edenkrans and Luigi Lessa of Marcus & Millichap marketed the property on behalf of the seller, E Homes Realty, LLC, and procured the buyer, Richardson Green. The property, located at 30 Log Bridge Road #303, is a 12,975-square-foot industrial condominium on a 13.51-acre lot. The property is split into a 7,025-square-foot warehouse, a 2,975-square-foot showroom on the first floor and a 2,975-square-foot second-floor office space. It was built in 2016 and includes 25 parking spaces. The post Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap closed sale of 12,975-sf industrial condo, Middleton MA, for $2.525M.
North Shore Boston; built 2016; warehouse, showroom, office; low market vacancy.
Asset sold to investor; core North Shore market tightness noted by broker.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of an industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Road, Middleton, Massachusetts, for $2.525 million. The 12,975-square-foot property (2016-built) includes warehouse, showroom, and office space on a 13.51-acre lot. An investor group purchased the asset, drawn by North Shore Boston's low industrial vacancy and property quality.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. Industrial market tightness on Boston's North Shore may signal broader supply constraints and land scarcity—potentially raising land costs for all residential and mixed-use development in the corridor, but the signal is indirect.

stories.title: CBRE Adds Freddie Mac’s Robert Koontz to Lead Multifamily Debt Capital Markets
stories.excerpt: CBRE has added Robert Koontz as head of multifamily debt capital markets after he led a similar role at Freddie Mac, the company announced Monday. Koontz, who spent 17 years at Freddie Mac and most recently served as head of multifamily capital markets at the government-sponsored enterprise, joined CBRE on July 6 as part of the brokerage’s efforts to expand its multifamily loan business. He will report to James Millon, co-head of capital markets, and work closely with Kelli Carhart, head of multifamily capital markets who worked with Koontz at Freddie Mac prior to joining CBRE in 2022. Koontz left Freddie Mac in November 2025. “Robert was a free agent and is super talented, and he’s one of those people that if they are available you figure out a way to bring them forward,” Carhart told Commercial Observer. “We’re bringing on somebody who is a platform builder, who culturally is a great fit, a great leader and a great visionary.’ At Freddie Mac, Koontz built out the GSE’s K-Deal CMBS program that launched in 2009. He will be focused at CBRE on expanding its balance sheet and risk transfer strategies which includes more bridge debt offerings such as bridge-to-agency loans. Koontz, who will split his time between Washington. D.C.. and New York, arrives at CBRE in a growth period for multifamily debt coming off a more than $30 billion origination output in 2025 with this year’s volume up 60 percent, according to Carhart. “The combination of origination capabilities, market intelligence, and an integrated platform across commercial real estate sales and investment banking creates a clear competitive advantage for clients,” Koontz said in a statement. “I look forward to working with this team to drive execution and expand what we can deliver for multifamily borrowers and investors across the country.” Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Robert Koontz (17 yrs Freddie Mac, head multifamily capital markets) joined CBRE July 6.
Will lead CBRE's multifamily debt capital markets; built Freddie Mac's K-Deal CMBS program.
Focus on balance sheet, risk transfer, bridge-to-agency strategies to expand multifamily lending.
stories.summary: CBRE hired Robert Koontz as head of multifamily debt capital markets, effective July 6. Koontz spent 17 years at Freddie Mac, most recently heading its multifamily capital markets division, where he developed the GSE's K-Deal CMBS program launched in 2009. At CBRE, he will expand balance sheet and risk transfer strategies, including bridge-to-agency debt offerings.
stories.impact: Senior capital markets hire signals CBRE's strategic bet on multifamily lending volume recovery and debt market expansion. Koontz's GSE pedigree and bridge-to-agency focus suggest lenders are preparing for renewed construction financing demand; developers should expect increased bridge debt availability and competitive pricing as brokerage platforms professionalize their debt origination. This also indicates multifamily lending remains a core growth market despite recent rate volatility.

stories.title: Rubicon Point Acquires Three-Building Office Complex Near Apple Headquarters
stories.excerpt: Rubicon Point Partners (RPP) has acquired Wolfe Square, a Class A, 117,795-square-foot multi-tenant office and medical campus located adjacent to Apple’s global headquarters in Cupertino. Although the buyer didn’t disclose the acquisition price, the Silicon Valley Business Journal recently reported it as more than $50 million. “Wolfe Square represents exactly the type of quality investment in a quality location that defines our investment strategy,” said Ani Vartanian, RPP co-Founder & managing director. “This is a community-critical building with institutional-grade tenancy, a proven rent roll, and a location that simply cannot be replicated. We are proud to steward this asset for years to come.” RPP plans to bring its UnCommon hospitality platform to the property. Will Connors, Dan Renz & Michael Manas of JLL represented the seller in the transaction. Bob Simpson and Erik Hallgrimson of Cushman & Wakefield serve as the leasing brokers for the property. The post Rubicon Point Acquires Three-Building Office Complex Near Apple Headquarters appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rubicon Point Partners acquired Wolfe Square, 117,795-sf office campus near Apple HQ, Cupertino.
Purchase price >$50M; Class A multi-tenant property with institutional tenancy.
RPP plans to activate UnCommon hospitality platform; signals office-to-mixed-use repositioning.
stories.summary: Rubicon Point Partners acquired Wolfe Square, a 117,795-square-foot Class A office and medical campus adjacent to Apple's global headquarters in Cupertino, for more than $50 million. The property features strong institutional tenancy and a proven rent roll. RPP plans to bring its UnCommon hospitality platform to the asset, suggesting mixed-use or lifestyle activation.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily signal, but Cupertino's office-to-mixed-use repositioning near Apple HQ reflects broader Silicon Valley trend of converting office to hospitality/lifestyle/residential hybrids. The move by a capital-backed investor toward experiential activation suggests single-use office is being re-underwritten; multifamily developers should monitor Cupertino/San Jose for office-building acquisition targets that could support conversion or ground-floor retail/hospitality to drive rents.

stories.title: Fashion Platform Orly Inks 20K-SF Office Lease at 20 West 33rd Street
stories.excerpt: Consumer products and fashion platform Orly has leased 20,014 square feet of office space at 20 West 33rd Street in Midtown South, according to the landlord broker. The deal, first reported by Traded, was signed with landlord JLA Home two weeks ago. Asking rent for the 10-year lease was $55 per square foot. Orly, founded in 1980 and also known as Orly-Corp., currently manages a portfolio of over 20 brands, ranging from footwear and apparel to household products and travel accessories. Its partnerships include Aeropostale, Lucky Brand and Nine West. Orly expects to move into its new, larger space early next year, according to a post on the company’s LinkedIn page. The company’s website locates its current headquarters one block north of their new lease, at 15 West 34th Street. Representatives of Orly could not immediately be reached for comment. LSL Advisors’ Wayne Siegel, Daniel Lolai and Zach Sarraf represented the tenant in the negotiations, while Lincoln Property Company’s Jeffrey Rosenblatt and Christina De Jesus worked on behalf of JLA. An entity of JLA, a home furnishing company, purchased its office condos at 20 West 33rd Street in 2022. Other tenants at the 13-story property include home and houseware company Vanderbilt Home Collections, restaurant Turntable Jazz Chicken and painting services provider JB Painters. Spokespeople for LSL Advisors and JLA Home did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Orly (fashion/consumer platform) leased 20,014-sf office at 20 West 33rd St., Midtown South.
10-year lease at $55/sf asking; 20+ brands portfolio (Aeropostale, Lucky Brand, Nine West).
Move-in early 2025; tenant upsizing from location one block north.
stories.summary: Orly, a fashion and consumer products platform managing 20+ brands (Aeropostale, Lucky Brand, Nine West), leased 20,014 square feet of office space at 20 West 33rd Street in Midtown South on a 10-year term at $55 per square foot. The company is relocating from nearby 15 West 34th Street and expects occupancy in early 2025.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. Office leasing in Midtown South reflects modest tenant stability; no signal of broader demand shifts affecting residential markets.

stories.title: Hackman’s Television City Heads Toward Sale as Studio Distress Grips Hollywood
stories.excerpt: Television City, a landmark studio in Los Angeles that has been home to productions ranging from “The Price Is Right” to “American Idol,” is poised to hit the market in another blow to owner and studio mogul Hackman Capital Partners (HCP). A lender group led by Deutsche Bank filed a notice of default last month alleging Hackman owes more than $357 million on the 25-acre property in the Fairfax District, according to the Los Angeles Times. The default follows months of negotiations between Hackman and its lenders as the company weighs alternatives, and it’s another marquee studio property in HCP’s industry-leading portfolio to fall into distress. It’s also the latest chapter in the unraveling of the industry’s bet that streaming would fuel an expansion and an extension of studio real estate demand. Culver City-based HCP over the past decade assembled the largest portfolio of studio properties, including a 2019 deal for $750 million to acquire Television City with its partner Affinius Capital (formerly Square Mile Capital) from CBS. HCP also planned a roughly $1 billion redevelopment that would add nearly 1 million square feet of offices, soundstages, production facilities and retail. But show business, and HCP’s portfolio, were significantly hamstrung by the post-pandemic slowdown, the 2023 writers and actors strikes, rising interest rates and studio consolidation. Earlier this year, Goldman Sachs took control of the 1.2 million-square-foot Radford Studio Center — where HCP also planned a $1 billion addition with another 1 million square feet of soundstage space — after the firm defaulted on a $1.1 billion mortgage. The property is now under contract to be sold to Netflix for roughly $400 million, a fraction of the nearly $1.9 billion Hackman and its partners paid in 2021. Meanwhile, Deutsche Bank has also been marketing HCP’s Manhattan Beach Studios, and has moved against other Hackman-backed assets in L.A. and New York. Television City borders Caruso’s The Grove an
bullets: Television City (25-acre studio, Fairfax District, LA) faces $357M+ default notice from Deutsche Bank.
Hackman Capital Partners (studio portfolio owner) in distress after streaming demand collapse.
Represents industry-wide studio real estate unwinding; another HCP marquee property at risk.
stories.summary: Television City, a landmark 25-acre studio lot in Los Angeles' Fairfax District (home to 'The Price Is Right,' 'American Idol'), faces a notice of default from a Deutsche Bank-led lender group alleging over $357 million in arrears. Owner Hackman Capital Partners assembled the industry's largest studio portfolio but is now unwinding after streaming failed to sustain expected demand growth. This follows months of negotiations as HCP weighs alternatives.
stories.impact: LA studio distress—particularly HCP's portfolio unraveling—signals potential large-scale land repositioning in Fairfax and adjacent industrial corridors. If Television City or similar studio campuses are liquidated, 20-50 acre parcels could become available for mixed-use or multifamily redevelopment. Multifamily developers should monitor HCP's asset sales and LA County industrial markets for distressed bulk land plays; regulatory zoning for studios may ease to adaptive reuse, creating opportunities for dense residential conversion.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas –– but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Soho Development sued Two Roads over $300M Four Seasons Bahamas condo project exclusion.
Judge dismissed lawsuit; Soho claims incorrect complaint addressed; litigation ongoing.
67-unit Ocean Club Residences on Paradise Island; Access Industries co-developer; presales underway.
stories.summary: Soho Development, led by Delray Beach attorney Roger Stein, sued Two Roads Development alleging exclusion from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas despite Soho providing contracts and confidential information. A judge dismissed the suit, but Soho claims the wrong complaint was addressed and plans to appeal. Two Roads and Access Real Estate (Len Blavatnik's firm) are developing the 67-unit Ocean Club Residences on Paradise Island with presales underway and 2028 delivery expected.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to U.S. multifamily development. Litigation around a Bahamas condo project signals reputational and operational risk for Two Roads in South Florida markets, but does not materially affect rental housing supply, demand, or capital availability in core U.S. multifamily metros.

stories.title: Blake Poston jumps from the gridiron to the high-rise with plans for a $100M Uptown Dallas apartment project
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Poston (Cover2 Capital) planning $100M, 20-story, 100-unit luxury rental at 5220 McKinney Ave., Uptown Dallas.
Ground-break targeted late 2025; average unit size 1,800 sf; includes 3 penthouses.
Cover2 also behind 297-unit Wheelhouse (North Richland Hills) and planned Greerhouse (Anna, TX).
stories.summary: Blake Poston, founder of Cover2 Capital and former SMU football player, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment high-rise in Uptown Dallas at 5220 McKinney Avenue. The project targets 100 units with an average size of 1,800 square feet and three penthouses, with ground-break planned for late 2025. Cover2 is also active in the market with the 297-unit Wheelhouse development in North Richland Hills and the planned Greerhouse in Anna.
stories.impact: Strong signal of developer capital and momentum in Uptown Dallas. Cover2's pipeline growth (The Savannah, Wheelhouse, Greerhouse) and large average unit sizing (1,800 sf) suggest competition among multifamily operators is shifting upscale and boutique-focused, not value-oriented. This may indicate rent power and absorption remain strong in Dallas, but it also signals capital concentration in luxury; value/workforce developers should monitor whether luxury oversupply emerges and compresses rents in secondary tiers.

stories.title: South Florida Dirt: A timeline of the Vacchi vs. Stern legal battle
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Stern teamed up with Italian investor and TikToker Gianluca Vacchi in 2024.  At the time, Stern said that Vacchi “understands new media and how to cut through the noise on social media in a way most real estate people don’t,” calling him an effective marketer and tastemaker. Online to his 22.1 million Instagram followers and 22.3 million TikTok followers, Vacchi has showcased his jetsetting lifestyle, workouts, cold plunges, and maybe most frequently, his dancing.  Two years ago, Stern and Vacchi said they were 50-50 partners on a $4 billion pipeline of JDS Development’s projects (Stern’s firm). That included […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Michael Stern (JDS Development) and Gianluca Vacchi (TikToker, 44M followers) partnership imploding.
Vacchi suing Stern for alleged Ponzi scheme, misrepresentation; $4B pipeline at risk.
Affects Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, Dolce & Gabbana 888 Brickell, 1250 West Avenue (Miami Beach).
stories.summary: Developer Michael Stern and Italian investor/TikToker Gianluca Vacchi, who partnered in 2024 as 50-50 collaborators on a $4 billion JDS Development pipeline, are now in litigation. Vacchi has accused Stern of running a Ponzi scheme and misrepresenting project status while diverting his capital. Key projects tied up include Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, Dolce & Gabbana-branded 888 Brickell, and 1250 West Avenue in Miami Beach.
stories.impact: South Florida luxury condo market exposure to founder/operator distress. While JDS is seeking new debt and Jeff Soffer partnership for Mercedes-Benz Places, litigation and leadership turmoil signal elevated execution risk on large Brickell/Miami Beach projects. Multifamily developers should monitor whether distressed condo inventory cascades into the rental market, or whether debt restructuring/new partners stabilize supply. Vacchi litigation may also chill celebrity/influencer capital partnerships in real estate development—a growing trend.

stories.title: Mark Cuban takes Adelson to court on Mavs plans for new arena
stories.excerpt: Who could’ve guessed that selling a basketball team would change who’s in charge? Apparently not Mark Cuban.  After hinting at his regret over selling the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands on a podcast in March, the billionaire businessman isn’t just going public with his concerns about the team’s new ownership–he’s taking them to court.  Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the team to Miriam Adelson’s casino company in 2023, claims in a new court filing that he was shut out of decisions about the team’s new arena, the Dallas Morning News reported. He’s asking the court to grant […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mark Cuban suing Las Vegas Sands (Mavs owner) over arena/Valley View Mall development exclusion.
Cuban claims verbal agreement to oversee operations; wants review of property purchase contract.
Casino arena contingent on Texas legalizing gambling; development path uncertain.
stories.summary: Mark Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the Dallas Mavericks to Miriam Adelson's Las Vegas Sands in 2023, is suing to gain review rights over the team's planned new arena at Valley View Mall. Cuban claims a verbal agreement entitled him to oversee operations while Sands pursued casino development. The project is contingent on Texas legalizing casino gambling, a legislative effort that has repeatedly stalled despite lobbying and campaign spending.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. However, the Valley View Mall redevelopment (if arena/casino advances) could catalyze broader Uptown Dallas mixed-use intensification and drive residential demand nearby. The litigation's outcome may delay or derail the project, reducing demand signals in that submarket; multifamily developers should monitor the legal and legislative status of Texas casino legalization before aggressive Uptown/Valley View corridor plays.

stories.title: Nonprofit pitches housing conversion at Palo Alto site with SB 79 proposal
stories.excerpt: A small office building in Palo Alto could give way to a multifamily building not far from one of the city’s Caltrain stops.  San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed the plan to erect a six-story building at 555 College Avenue with 70 apartments and roughly 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, Mercury News reported. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million, according to documents filed with the Santa Clara County Recorder’s Office.  The building would consist of five stories of housing above a ground floor that could accommodate retail, dining or other commercial uses. The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Minority Television Project (nonprofit) proposed 70-unit, 6-story apartment at 555 College Ave., Palo Alto.
Filed under SB 79 provisions; near California Avenue Caltrain station (transit-oriented development).
Also pursuing 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Avenue.
stories.summary: San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project, which purchased 555 College Avenue for $7.1 million in 2023, has proposed a six-story, 70-unit apartment building with 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space. The project leverages Senate Bill 79, legislation allowing taller housing within a half-mile of qualifying transit hubs (in this case, California Avenue Caltrain station). The nonprofit is also pursuing a separate 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Avenue using SB 79 approvals.
stories.impact: SB 79 enablement and nonprofit-led transit-oriented development signal California's regulatory push to densify near transit and unlock supply in supply-constrained corridors. Palo Alto's entitlement/zoning historically restrictive; SB 79 provides a pathway to density for nonprofits and public-benefit developers. For-profit developers should monitor California legislative momentum toward state-level zoning override and transit-oriented density mandates—this model may expand to other metros, shifting entitlement risk/opportunity from municipal to state level.

stories.title: Steel IQ’s 3D-printed steel could slash builds from months to weeks
stories.excerpt: The new Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ believes that its 3D digital extrusion and printing is the sustainable future for affordable housing in America.  One of the major problems in the United States’ development pipeline is a lack of skilled construction workers. Steel IQ’s 3D printed steel frames may cost more to install, but require fewer skilled workers and cut down on framing timelines. The process uses a  digital model of a building, finds its dimensions with AI assistance and prints it in steel, down to the holes for the screws, according to the Dallas Morning News. Steel IQ’s founder […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Steel IQ (Lake Dallas startup) commercializing 3D-printed steel framing; cuts labor and timeline.
AI-assisted digital models; process reduces waste, assembly time vs. wood framing.
Tested on luxury residential (Infinity mansion, Frisco); cost offset by efficiency gains.
stories.summary: Steel IQ, a Lake Dallas startup, is commercializing 3D digital extrusion and printing of steel building frames to accelerate construction timelines and reduce skilled labor requirements. The process uses AI-assisted digital modeling to design and print steel frames with pre-cut holes, enabling on-site assembly similar to modular construction. While steel costs more than wood, efficiency, waste reduction, and labor savings offset the premium. The technology has been tested on luxury residential projects, including the Infinity mansion in Frisco.
stories.impact: Construction technology innovation addressing acute labor shortage in residential development. Steel IQ's approach may improve feasibility for affordable housing projects constrained by framing labor availability. If 3D steel printing scales and costs decline, it could compress development timelines and reduce construction contingency; multifamily developers should pilot and monitor adoption rates, as earlier delivery and reduced labor variance improve cash flow and risk profiles. Cost parity with wood framing is the key adoption threshold.
